result.question:
Residual scope of a prior survey (Issue #265) for a SMALL human-orchestrated AI agent/role team (1 human "message bus" + 3-4 personas: PM/Scientist/Developer/Memory-Manager) doing research-bioinformatics software work on a shared GitHub board. A 2026-07 governance report ALREADY covers, and you should NOT re-cover: multi-agent failure modes (MAST taxonomy), human-in-the-loop placement, review-debt / WIP-limit dials, external-memory importance. A landscape doc ALREADY covers the proprietary systems: Anthropic Managed Agents, OpenAI Symphony, Sakana Fugu, GitHub Copilot desktop, Microsoft Conductor. Focus ONLY on these two under-covered areas:

(1) Developer-facing multi-agent ORCHESTRATION FRAMEWORKS as of 2025-2026 - specifically LangGraph/LangChain agents, CrewAI, Microsoft AutoGen (AgentChat / v0.4+), OpenAI Agents SDK (and Swarm predecessor), and any notable newer entrant. For EACH: its orchestration model (graph vs role/crew vs handoff-based), maturity + real adoption signal (GitHub stars/production use), and critically whether its coordination primitives add anything over our existing design (a shared human-read board as the coordination substrate + a human as the message bus + on-disk role memory). Which, if any, would a team like ours actually adopt, and for which specific coordination beat (handoff, dispatch, conflict-resolution)?

(2) Multi-agent-system RELIABILITY BENCHMARKS and EVALS - what benchmarks/evals exist for measuring multi-agent reliability/correctness (e.g. tau-bench, AgentBench, MAST-derived harnesses, others)? Are they meaningful and predictive, or gameable? What would a team like ours actually use to evaluate the reliability of OUR own setup, beyond the prior report's "measure verification lead time + rework rate" recommendation?

For each sub-question deliver concrete evidence with source links, explicitly flag what transfers to a small research-bioinformatics team vs what is large-software-org-specific, and name any low-risk adoption increment worth a follow-up Issue.
result.summary: For a small human-orchestrated persona team, the developer-facing orchestration frameworks (Swarm/OpenAI Agents SDK, AutoGen v0.4+, and by extension CrewAI/LangGraph) converge on two coordination primitives - graph/pub-sub routing and handoff-via-tool-call delegation - but their defining move is to REPLACE the human-as-message-bus with a machine runtime and to treat the human as just another symmetric pub-sub endpoint, which is the inverse of our design rather than an upgrade to it; none of their primitives add anything over a shared human-read board + human bus + on-disk role memory for a 1-human/3-4-persona team, so the low-risk increment is borrowing a NAMED pattern (a delegate/handoff convention, not a framework). On the reliability-eval side, the strongest and most transferable finding is that single-run benchmark scores (pass@1) systematically and materially overestimate real reliability (GPT-4o 61% pass@1 collapses to ~25% pass^8; benchmark-design flaws alone swing scores up to 100% relative, and automated red-teaming reward-hacked 9/10 popular benchmarks to near-perfect without solving a single task), so headline agent-benchmark numbers are not predictive as-is. What transfers to us is not the large-org benchmarks themselves but their underlying metrics and methods: repeat-run consistency (pass^k), partial-credit/graceful-degradation scoring, and full-execution-trace failure attribution (which improved attribution accuracy up to 76% over output-only observation) - all of which extend the prior report's "verification lead time + rework rate" with cheap, board-native instrumentation. A notable negative result worth heeding: a naive episodic-memory scaffold universally hurt long-horizon reliability across all 10 models tested, cautioning against assuming more memory automatically improves reliability.

findings.claim: OpenAI Swarm is an explicitly experimental/educational framework, not production-ready, and has been superseded by the production-oriented OpenAI Agents SDK; Swarm itself is not an adoption candidate.
findings.evidence: OpenAI's own Swarm README titles it 'Swarm (experimental, educational)' and states 'Swarm is now replaced by the OpenAI Agents SDK, which is a production-ready evolution of Swarm... We recommend migrating to the Agents SDK for all production use cases.' Corroborated by multiple independent migration guides (March 2025 supersession). Merges claims [0] and [5], both 3-0.

findings.claim: Swarm's (and by inheritance the Agents SDK's) coordination model is handoff-based: an agent transfers control by having a called function RETURN another Agent object, optionally bundling return values + context updates in one Result object, giving dynamic decentralized routing among specialized agents with no central router.
findings.evidence: README: 'An Agent can hand off to another Agent by returning it in a function' (pattern `def transfer_to_sales(): return sales_agent`); can 'update the context_variables by returning a more complete Result object' that carries a value, an agent, and context together. 'An Agent... can at any point choose to hand off a conversation to another Agent.' This is the single most-cited Swarm primitive. Merges claims [1] and [6], both 3-0. Relevant coordination beat for us: HANDOFF.

findings.claim: Microsoft AutoGen (v0.4+) implements the same delegation-via-tool-call handoff mechanism that Swarm introduced: agents delegate to other agents using a special 'delegate/handoff' tool call rather than through a central router.
findings.evidence: AutoGen's own core docs: 'Handoff is a multi-agent design pattern introduced by OpenAI in an experimental project called Swarm. The key idea is to let agent delegate tasks to other agents using a special tool call.' Docs explicitly contrast this against 'a central orchestrator such as in SelectorGroupChat', confirming the no-central-router framing. Self-attribution by Microsoft = highest-quality source for what AutoGen reimplements. Claim [7], 3-0.

findings.claim: AutoGen's handoff coordination is decentralized and machine-mediated: each agent autonomously decides whether to delegate via delegate tools, routing occurs through topic-based pub-sub subscriptions managed by the agent runtime, and there is no central ROUTER/orchestrator agent making routing decisions.
findings.evidence: Docs: an AIAgent 'can use regular tools directly or delegate tasks to other agents using delegate_tools'; each agent 'subscribes to topic type agent_topic_type'; on a handoff tool call it 'delegates... by publishing a UserTask message to the topic specified in the tool call result.' Claim [8], 2-1. Nuance from verifier: the pub-sub bus is itself the runtime, so 'no central message bus' is loose - the precise, well-supported reading is 'no central router AGENT; routing DECISIONS are decentralized.' Confidence medium due to split vote and this semantic looseness.

findings.claim: AutoGen treats a human as a symmetric pub-sub message ENDPOINT (a HumanAgent subscribing/publishing on topics), NOT as a coordinator - the direct inverse of our design where the human is the central message bus. This means frameworks do not add a human-as-bus primitive; they remove it.
findings.evidence: Docs: 'The HumanAgent class subscribes to agent_topic_type to receive messages and publishes to user_topic_type to send messages'; it is 'a proxy for the human... a message endpoint/participant, not a central coordinator... operating through the same event-driven pub-sub infrastructure as AI agents, rather than as a special central authority.' Claim [9], 3-0. This is the load-bearing adoption finding: for a 1-human/N-persona team whose entire design is human-as-bus + human-read board, these frameworks' primitives are architecturally orthogonal (they automate away the human coordinator), so they add nothing to our coordination substrate and would require inverting our model to adopt wholesale. The transferable increment is a NAMED handoff/delegate convention on the board, not a framework runtime.

findings.claim: Single-attempt success (pass@1) is structurally blind to reliability and single-run agent benchmarks (ToolBench, AgentBench, API-Bank) systematically overestimate production reliability; multi-trial consistency is far lower - GPT-4o scores 61% pass@1 on retail (tau-bench) tasks but only ~25% pass^8, i.e. ~75% chance of at least one failure across 8 runs.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2603.29231: 'GPT-4o achieves 61% pass@1 on retail agent tasks but only 25% pass@8 - meaning... the probability of at least one failure approaches 75%.' arXiv:2601.06112 (ReliabilityBench): 'ToolBench and AgentBench provide single-run success rates that systematically overestimate production reliability'; '60% pass@1 may exhibit only 25% consistency across multiple trials.' Figure traces to Sierra's tau-bench pass^k metric (independently corroborated). Merges claims [2] (3-0) and [12] (2-1). HIGHLY TRANSFERABLE to us: repeat-run consistency (pass^k) is the cheapest reliability instrument and directly extends the prior report's rework-rate recommendation - run the same persona beat/task N times and count all-succeed, not once.

findings.claim: A naive episodic-memory scaffold UNIVERSALLY hurt long-horizon reliability: episodic memory augmentation produced negative or neutral Graceful Degradation Score for all 10 models evaluated - strong evidence against naive episodic memory as a reliability intervention.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2603.29231: 'memory scaffolds universally hurt long-horizon GDS (negative or neutral for all 10 models), providing strong evidence against naive episodic memory as a reliability intervention.' Table 13: 4 models neutral, 6 hurt (worst -0.14), none positive. Claim [3], 3-0. CAVEAT for us: our on-disk role memory is curated/structured, NOT a naive episodic scratchpad, so this is a caution not a condemnation - but it directly warns against assuming 'more agent memory = more reliable.' Single non-peer-reviewed preprint; the 'naive' scope-limit is the authors' own.

findings.claim: A four-metric reliability-science framework is proposed as a first-class evaluation dimension alongside capability: Reliability Decay Curve (how pass^k degrades with task duration), Variance Amplification Factor (how duration amplifies stochastic failure), Graceful Degradation Score (partial-credit for partially-completed long tasks), and Meltdown Onset Point (behavioral collapse via sliding-window entropy over tool-call sequences).
findings.evidence: arXiv:2603.29231 abstract defines all four metrics verbatim (RDC, VAF, GDS, MOP). Claim [4], 3-0. TRANSFERABLE method (not the harness): GDS-style partial-credit scoring of a partially-completed long persona task, and MOP-style detection of behavioral collapse, are concepts a small team can apply qualitatively without heavy infra. Single preprint source; concepts more transferable than the formal metrics.

findings.claim: Agent benchmarks are frequently and materially unreliable/gameable as-is: design flaws under/overestimate performance by up to 100% relative; named mainstream benchmarks have concrete validity bugs (SWE-bench Verified insufficient test cases; TAU-bench counts empty responses as success); agents shortcut tasks (searching HuggingFace for the benchmark instead of solving it); tau2-bench itself shipped 75+ task-quality fixes; and automated red-teaming (BenchJack) reward-hacked 9/10 popular benchmarks to near-perfect scores WITHOUT solving a single task.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2507.02825: flaws cause 'under- or overestimation... by up to 100% in relative terms'; 'SWE-bench Verified uses insufficient test cases, while TAU-bench counts empty responses as successful' (10 benchmarks audited, 7 outcome-validity flaws). arXiv:2510.11977 (HAL, 21,730 rollouts): agents 'searching for the benchmark on HuggingFace instead of solving a task, or misusing credit cards.' arXiv:2605.12673 (BenchJack): reward-hacking exploits 'achieve near-perfect scores on most of the benchmarks without solving a single task' (219 flaws; near-perfect on 9 of 10, e.g. a 9-line conftest.py exploit → 100% on SWE-bench Verified/Terminal-Bench). tau2-bench release notes: 'τ³-bench v1.0.0... 75+ fixes.' Merges claims [11],[15],[16],[21],[22] (all 3-0) and [23] (2-1). BOTTOM LINE for us: do NOT treat headline agent-benchmark numbers as predictive of our setup's reliability. LARGE-ORG-SPECIFIC: the benchmarks themselves (retail/airline/SWE tasks) don't match research-bioinformatics work.

findings.claim: tau2-bench evaluates agent reliability via three-party tool-agent-user interaction: a user simulator with its OWN tools interacts with an agent that must follow policy and use tools to complete tasks (dual-control environment).
findings.evidence: Paper 'τ²-Bench: Evaluating Conversational Agents in a Dual-Control Environment' (arXiv:2506.07982). Repo: each domain specifies 'A policy that the agent must follow,' 'tools that the agent can use,' and 'Optionally: user tools for the user simulator.' Claim [10], 3-0. Caveat: conflates original τ-bench (two-party, no user tools) with tau2's dual-control addition; dual-control is fully exercised only in the telecom domain. LARGE-ORG-SPECIFIC domains (retail/airline/telecom); the three-party structure is more relevant than the tasks.

findings.claim: LLM-simulated users are unreliable proxies for real humans in agentic evals, so single-simulator benchmark scores are not robust: on tau-Bench retail with the agent fixed (GPT-4o), swapping only the user-simulator LLM shifts measured agent success by ~9 percentage points (Sonnet 3.7 67.0% vs Sonnet 4.5 75.9%); simulated users are miscalibrated against real humans (ECE 15.1), underestimating success on the hardest tasks and overestimating on moderate ones.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2601.17087 ('Lost in Simulation'): Table 1 confirms 67.0%±3.3 (Sonnet 3.7 simulator) vs 75.9%±3.5 (Sonnet 4.5 simulator) = 8.9pp with GPT-4o agent fixed; '45.2% success with US participants and ECE_Human-LLM of 15.1... substantial miscalibration'; simulations 'underestimate agent success on the hardest tasks (30.8% human) while overestimating on moderate tasks (39.0% human).' Merges claims [19] and [20], both 3-0. Implication: an eval whose 'judge' or 'user' is itself an LLM inherits the judge's biases - relevant to any LLM-as-user or LLM-as-judge harness we might build.

findings.claim: ReliabilityBench measures agent reliability along three composable, production-stress dimensions unified in a Reliability Surface R(k,ε,λ): consistency (repeatability across identical runs, k), robustness (handling semantically-equivalent but syntactically varied instructions, ε), and fault tolerance (recovery from infrastructure failures, λ).
findings.evidence: arXiv:2601.06112 defines R(k,ε,λ)=E[pass^k(perturb_ε(τ),A) | fault profile λ], 'a three-dimensional evaluation surface that unifies consistency, robustness, and fault tolerance metrics' (1,280 episodes, 96.9%→88.1% degradation under chaos-engineering fault injection). Claim [13], 3-0 vote but single-author preprint (medium confidence on source quality). The three-axis decomposition (consistency / robustness / fault-tolerance) is a clean, transferable mental model for stress-testing our persona setup - e.g. rerun a beat with reworded instructions (robustness) and under a simulated tool/network failure (fault tolerance).

findings.claim: HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard) is a standardized evaluation harness addressing the reproducibility/infrastructure gap - it runs parallel agent evaluations across hundreds of VMs, cutting evaluation time from weeks to hours - because current agent evaluations have systemic flaws that make evaluation METHODOLOGY, not model capability, the primary bottleneck to reliable deployment.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2510.11977 (Kapoor et al., 30 authors incl. Narayanan/Liang/Song/Bommasani, ICLR 2026): 'a standardized evaluation harness that orchestrates parallel evaluations across hundreds of Azure VMs, reducing evaluation time from weeks to hours'; 'AI agent evaluations suffer from many challenges that undermine our understanding of how well agents really work'; concludes 'evaluation methodology, not model capability, constitutes the primary bottleneck.' 21,730 rollouts, ~$40K. Merges claims [14] and [15], both 3-0. LARGE-ORG-SPECIFIC infrastructure (hundreds of VMs, $40K) - NOT for us to run - but the thesis (standardize your eval method before trusting scores) directly supports our 'measure our own setup' approach.

findings.claim: Failure ATTRIBUTION is a distinct, high-value evaluation axis: TraceElephant benchmarks who-and-what-step caused a failure in LLM multi-agent systems using FULL execution traces (inputs + context, not just outputs); providing full traces improves attribution accuracy by up to 76% over output-only observation.
findings.evidence: arXiv:2604.22708 ('Seeing the Whole Elephant', ACL 2026): introduces TraceElephant 'for failure attribution with full execution traces and reproducible environments'; 'existing benchmarks only examine agent outputs, missing the inputs and context developers actually use for debugging' (220 failure traces annotated with responsible component + decisive step); 'full traces improve attribution accuracy by up to 76% over a partial-observation counterpart' (76% = max, step-level; 22% agent-level). Merges claims [17] and [18], both 3-0. MOST DIRECTLY TRANSFERABLE reliability practice for us: when a persona beat fails, capture the FULL trace (inputs/context/handoffs on the board), not just the final output - the prior report's 'rework rate' becomes far more actionable when you can attribute WHICH persona and WHICH step caused the rework.

result.caveats: Source-quality caveats: several reliability findings rest on single 2025-2026 arXiv preprints that are not peer-reviewed (2603.29231 ReliabilityBench-adjacent, 2601.06112 ReliabilityBench is single-author, 2605.12673 BenchJack), so their specific numbers should be treated as directional rather than settled; the ROBUST, multiply-corroborated core is the qualitative finding that single-run scores overestimate real reliability (this traces to Sierra's tau-bench pass^k and is echoed across HAL, 2507.02825, and BenchJack). Time-sensitivity: the framework landscape (Swarm→Agents SDK, AutoGen v0.4) moves fast and adoption/star signals shift monthly; the architectural CONCLUSION (frameworks automate the human coordinator away, so their primitives are orthogonal to a human-as-bus design) is stable, but specific framework maturity claims should be re-checked before any adoption decision. Two votes were 2-1 (claim [8] AutoGen 'no central message bus' - the precise reading is 'no central router agent', and claim [23] BenchJack - could not independently confirm TAU-Bench/GAIA specifically were among the 9 near-perfect-hacked vs the 1 not); I lowered the relevant findings to medium or flagged the nuance inline. Coverage gaps in the underlying research: CrewAI and LangGraph were named in the question but produced NO surviving verified claims - the framework evidence that survived is entirely Swarm/Agents-SDK and AutoGen, so conclusions about CrewAI's crew/role model and LangGraph's explicit-graph model are inferred by analogy, not directly evidenced here. The Promptfoo claim was refuted (0-3), so no verified evidence exists on lightweight CI-native eval tooling. Nothing here directly benchmarks a research-bioinformatics workflow; all named benchmarks (retail/airline/telecom/SWE/web) are large-software-org domains, so their SCORES don't transfer - only their metrics and methods do.
openQuestions: Do CrewAI's role/crew orchestration and LangGraph's explicit-graph model offer any coordination primitive (e.g. a durable shared-state graph, or a role-crew delegation convention) that would genuinely improve on our human-read board - or, like Swarm/AutoGen, do they just relocate the human out of the coordinator seat? No verified evidence survived on these two despite being in scope.

What is the cheapest concrete pass^k-style instrument a 1-human team can actually run on its OWN persona beats (e.g. re-run the same handoff/dispatch task 3-5x and count all-succeed), and does it correlate with the prior report's rework-rate metric in practice?

Since a naive episodic-memory scaffold universally hurt long-horizon reliability, does our CURATED on-disk role memory measurably help or hurt - i.e. is there an A/B (memory-on vs memory-off) worth running on a few representative beats before assuming our memory layer is net-positive for reliability?

Is a lightweight full-execution-TRACE capture on the board (inputs + context + handoff chain per failed beat, TraceElephant-style) worth a follow-up Issue, given the up-to-76% attribution-accuracy gain over output-only observation - and what is the minimum trace fidelity that still lets us attribute which persona/step caused a rework?

refuted.claim: Promptfoo's evaluation scope is prompt quality and security red-teaming; it does not provide any multi-agent-system reliability evaluation, so it does not address the multi-agent reliability-benchmark gap directly.
